stem: Identify the vertebral level of the given anatomical cross-section:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
Visible Structures:
• 1. Both right and left lungs.
• 2. Liver
• 3. Esophagus
• 4. Spleen
At the T10 level, both lungs are visible, indicating the presence of thoracic structures. The liver, esophagus, and spleen are also visible, which is consistent with this level At T11 and T12, the lungs start to taper off and may not be fully visible. The absence of the colon is also noted at this level, confirming T10 as the correct answer.

Other Options:
Option B: T12
Visible Structures:
• 1. Lungs are not visible.
• 2. Liver
• 3. Esophagus
• 4. Spleen
At T12, the lungs are typically not visible, as they taper off around the lower thoracic levels. The presence of the liver, esophagus, and spleen can be noted, but the key distinguishing feature is the absence of lung visibility, which rules out T12 for the given Image.
Option C: T11
Visible Structures:
• 1. Lungs start to taper off and may not be fully visible.
• 2. Liver
• 3. Esophagus
• 4. Spleen
Similar to T12, at T11 the lungs are less visible as they begin to taper off. This is inconsistent with the observation that both lungs are visible in the given cross-section, thus ruling out T11.
Option D: T9
Visible Structures:
• 1. Both right and left lungs.
• 2. Liver (partially).
• 3. Esophagus.
• 4. Spleen is not typically visible.
At T9, both lungs are visible, and the superior aspect of the liver starts to appear. However, the spleen is usually not visible at this level, which contradicts the given cross-section details.

stem: The bone shown in the image is not associated with which of the following types of joints?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
The image shown above is of vertebra. Synchondrosis is a type of joint where the bones are connected by hyaline cartilage. These joints are usually temporary and found in growing bones, such as the epiphyseal plates (growth plates) in children and the first sternocostal joint that unites the first rib to the sternum. Synchondrosis or primary cartilaginous joints are not present in the given image.

Other Options:
Option A: Syndesmosis
Definition: A syndesmosis is a type of fibrous joint where bones are connected by a ligament or a sheet of connective tissue. The supraspinous ligament, interspinous ligament, ligamentum flavum (connecting the lamina of adjacent bones), posterior longitudinal ligament, anterior longitudinal ligament, these ligaments connect the adjacent vertebrae forming syndesmosis.
Option B: Symphysis
Definition: A symphysis is a type of cartilaginous joint where the bones are connected by fibrocartilage.
• Examples:
• 1. The pubic symphysis.
• 2. The intervertebral discs.
Option C: Plane Synovial Joint
A plane synovial Joint, also known as a gliding Joint, involves flat or nearly flat articular surfaces that allow bones to slide over one another. The joint between the superior articular facet and inferior articular facet is a plane synovial joint and thus makes it an incorrect choice.

stem: Identify the arterial supply of the anatomical region of the heart shown in the image:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
Anterior Interventricular Artery (Left Anterior Descending Artery):
• The anterior interventricular artery, also known as the left anterior descending (LAD) artery, is responsible for supplying the anterior part of the Interventricular septum and the anterior walls of both ventricles.
Associated Regions:
• 1. Anterior wall of the left ventricle.
• 2. Part of the right ventricle.
• 3. Anterior two-thirds of the interventricular septum.

Other Options:
Option B: Left Circumflex Artery
Supplies the lateral and posterior walls of the left ventricle and part of the left atrium.
Associated Regions:
• 1. Lateral wall of the left ventricle.
• 2. Posterior wall of the left ventricle.
• 3. Part of the left atrium.
Option C: Left Marginal Artery
Function: Supplies the left margin (or border) of the heart. Details: It is a branch of the left circumflex artery and runs along the margin of the left ventricle.
Associated Regions:
• 1. Lateral wall of the left ventricle, especially along the margin.
Option D: Right Coronary Artery (RCA)
• Supplies the right atrium, right ventricle, and part of the left ventricle, as well as the sinoatrial (SA) and atrioventricular (AV) nodes.
Associated Regions:
• 1. Right atrium.
• 2. Right ventricle.
• 3. Inferior wall of the left ventricle.
• 4. SA and AV nodes.

stem: Which of the following statements is true regarding the anatomy of the breast?
options: The superior medial quadrant has more tissue.
The axillary tail of Spence crosses the anterior axillary fold.
The nipple is located at the level of the fourth Intercostal space in most women.
subject: Anatomy, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
The axillary tail of Spence is an extension of the breast tissue that extends Into the axilla, and it does cross the anterior axillary fold.

Other Options:
Option A: The superior medial quadrant has more tissue: The superior lateral quadrant actually contains the most glandular tissue and is a common site for breast cancer.
Option B: There are 10 lobules present: The human breast typically has 15-20 lobules, not just 10.
Option D: The nipple is located at the level of the fourth Intercostal space in most women: While the nipple is commonly said to be at the level of the fourth intercostal space, this can vary greatly among individuals and is not a definitive anatomical landmark for all women.

stem: Patient presented to the OPD with complaints of Pain, pressure or a sense of fullness around the face, head and congested nose. Which of the following X-ray view is best for visualization of frontal sinuses?
subject: ENT, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• The Caldwell view (also known as the occipitofrontal view) is specifically designed to provide a clear image of the frontal sinuses. This view angles the X-ray beam to project the frontal sinuses without superimposition from other facial structures, making It ideal for assessing the frontal sinuses.

Other Options:
Option A: Lateral view: The lateral view is not ideal for visualizing the frontal sinuses. It is typically used to view the sphenoidal sinus, the nasopharynx and to assess any anteroposterior displacement of structures in the skull.
Option C: Waters view: The Waters view (also known as the occipitomental view) is best for visualizing the maxillary sinuses. It is used to see the orbits and the maxillary sinuses with minimal superimposition of the petrous ridges.
Option D: Towne view: The Towne view is primarily used for visualizing the occipital bone, the posterior cranial fossa and for assessing the foramen magnum. It is not optimal for visualizing the frontal sinuses.

stem: What is the most common type of hernia in females?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
Indirect inguinal hernias are indeed the most common type of hernia overall, including in females.
• This type of hernia occurs when abdominal contents protrude through the deep Inguinal ring and Into the Inguinal canal, often due to a congenital defect in the abdominal wall. Despite the anatomical predisposition in females for femoral hernias, Indirect Inguinal hernias still occur more frequently In both sexes.

Other Options:
Option A: Obturator hernia: These are quite rare and occur through the obturator foramen. They are typically found in older, multiparous women and are not the most common type of hernia in females.
Option B: Direct Inguinal hernia: Direct Inguinal hernias occur due to weakness in the abdominal wall in the Inguinal (Hesselbach's) triangle. These are more common in men and are not the most common type in females.
Option D: Femoral hernia: While femoral hernias are more common in females compared to males due to the wider female pelvis, they are still less common than Indirect Inguinal hernias in females.

stem: A 24 year old med school student was going under thyroid surgery. Patient was concerned about the risks of thyroid surgery. Which of the following steps in thyroid surgery is least likely to result in hoarseness of voice?
subject: Surgery, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• Ligation of the superior thyroid artery is a step in thyroid surgery that generally poses a lower risk of causing hoarseness of voice. This is because the superior laryngeal nerve, which can be affected during this procedure, primarily affects the cricothyrold muscle responsible for pitch modulation rather than causing hoarseness directly. Therefore, this step is least likely to cause hoarseness compared to other steps that have a direct impact on the recurrent laryngeal nerve.

Other Options:
Option A: Dissection of Beahrs triangle: Beahrs triangle is an area near the recurrent laryngeal nerve. Dissecting this region can also pose a risk to the nerve, leading to a higher likelihood of hoarseness of volce if the nerve is damaged.
Option B: Division of strap muscles: Dividing the strap muscles (sternothyroid and sternohyoid) is a common procedure, but while it is generally safe, It is not related to the risk of voice changes directly associated with nerve damage. However, compared to ligating the superior thyroid artery, it is more likely to be involved in complex dissection areas where nerves might be at risk.
Option C: Removal of tubercle of Zuckerkandl: This procedure is closely associated with the recurrent laryngeal nerve. The tubercle of Zuckerkandl is an anatomical landmark near the recurrent laryngeal nerve, and its removal carries a significant risk of nerve Injury, which can result in hoarseness of voice.

stem: Which of the following structure is not present in the floor of the Inferior horn of the lateral ventricle?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
The tail of the caudate nucleus is not present in the floor of the inferior horn of the lateral ventricle. The caudate nucleus is part of the basal ganglia, and its tail arches around and terminates in the roof of the Inferior horn of the lateral ventricle.

Other Options:
Option A: Hippocampus: The hippocampus is present in the floor of the inferior horn of the lateral ventricle. It is a key structure involved in memory and spatial navigation.
Option C: Fimbriae: The fimbriae of the hippocampus are present in the floor of the inferior horn of the lateral ventricle. They are bundles of axons that emerge from the hippocampus and converge to form the fornix.
Option D: Collateral eminence: The collateral eminence is a ridge on the floor of the inferior horn of the lateral ventricle caused by the underlying collateral sulcus. It is indeed present in the floor of the inferior horn.

stem: Which of the following vertebrae level corresponds to the ischial spine?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The ischial spine refers to the sharp bony prominence which protrudes from the posterior margin of the ischium. It separates the greater sciatic notch above from the lesser sciatic notch below.
• Despite commonly being referenced in relation to the S3-S5 vertebral level in some anatomical texts, in certain contexts or specific anatomical references, it may be aligned or associated with the tip of the coccyx.

Other Options:
Option A, C, & D:
Refer to the explanation of Option B.

stem: Identify the structure in the given radiology image?
subject: Anatomy, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
This is a common vascular anomaly where the right subclavian artery arises abnormally from the aortic arch and travels behind the esophagus to the right arm.

Other Options:
Option A: Ascending aorta
The ascending aorta is the initial section of the aorta arising from the heart and extending upwards.
• It would not have the characteristic path seen in an aberrant subclavian artery.
Option B: Aberrant left subclavian artery
• An aberrant left subclavian artery is a rare anomaly compared to the right.
It would arise from the right side and travel to the left, but this is not the typical presentation of aberrant subclavian arteries.
Option D: Main pulmonary artery
• The main pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs.
• Its course and branching are distinct and do not resemble the path of an aberrant subclavian artery.

stem:
Match the following with the most appropriate feature:
DiseaseFeature
1. Von Gierke'sa. Branched structure in liver biopsy
2. Pompe'sb. Glycogen deposition in lysosomes
3. McArdle'sc. Hypoglycemia with lactic acidosis

subject: Biochemistry

Solution
Von Glerke's Disease: Characterized by hypoglycemia, and lactic acidosis due to the deficiency of glucose-6- phosphatase.
Pompe's Disease: Characterized by glycogen deposition in lysosomes due to acid alpha-glucosidase deficiency.
• McArdle's Disease: Causes exercise Intolerance due to the lack of muscle glycogen phosphorylase.
Cori's Disease: Characterized by the accumulation of branched glycogen due to deficiency of debranching enzyme.

Other Options:

stem: Which of the following is not associated with post-transcriptional modification?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
Glycosylation is a post-translational modification that occurs after translation, not during post-transcriptional modification.

Other Options:
Option A: Methylation: Methylation can occur as a post-transcriptional modification, particularly in eukaryotic mRNA.
It can affect gene expression and mRNA stability.
Option B: Endonuclease cleavage: Endonuclease cleavage typically occurs during RNA processing, where introns are removed from the pre-mRNA to produce mature mRNA.
So, It is indeed associated with post-transcriptional modification.
Option C: 5' capping: 5' capping is a crucial post-transcriptional modification in eukaryotic mRNA, where a 7-methylguanosine cap is added to the 5' end.
This modification protects the mRNA from degradation and aids in translation initiation.

stem: A 34 year old mother was concerned about her child having lethargy, vomiting and abnormally rapid breathing. Which of the following deficiency is found in congenital lactic acidosis?
subject: Pediatrics, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Pyruvate dehydrogenase (PDH) is a crucial enzyme that converts pyruvate into acetyl-CoA, linking glycolysis to the citric acid cycle.
A deficiency in pyruvate dehydrogenase leads to congenital lactic acidosis.
Without functional PDH, pyruvate accumulates and is shunted to lactate production, leading to elevated lactate levels in the blood.

Other Options:
Option A: Transketolase
• Transketolase is an enzyme of the pentose phosphate pathway, which helps in the production of NADPH and ribose-5- phosphate.
• A deficiency in transketolase is not associated with congenital lactic acidosis.
Option B: Branched chain Ketoacid dehydrogenase
• This enzyme is involved in the catabolism of branched-chain amino acids (leucine, isoleucine, and valine).
• A deficiency in branched-chain ketoacid dehydrogenase leads to Maple Syrup Urine Disease (MSUD), not congenital lactic acidosis.
MSUD is characterized by the accumulation of branched-chain amino acids and their corresponding keto acids.
Option C: Isocitrate dehydrogenase
• Isocitrate dehydrogenase is an enzyme in the citric acid cycle that helps in the conversion of isocitrate to a-ketoglutarate, producing NADPH or NADH in the process.
• While Important in the citric acid cycle, a deficiency in isocitrate dehydrogenase is not typically associated with congenital lactic acidosis.

stem: Which type of mutation results in the reversal to the wild type of phenotype when the mutant gene is suppressed?
subject: Biochemistry, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
This refers to a suppressor mutation, where a second mutation occurs in a tRNA gene.
The mutant tRNA can recognize the mutant codon and insert the correct amino acid, thus restoring the wild-type phenotype despite the presence of the original mutation in the coding gene.
• This type of mutation effectively suppresses the impact of the original mutation.

Other Options:
Option A: Frameshift of the mutated gene
A frameshift mutation involves inserting or deleting a number of nucleotides that are not divisible by three.
This shifts the reading frame of the codons in the mRNA, often resulting in a completely nonfunctional protein.
• This type of mutation usually cannot be easily reversed by a single mutation and thus is unlikely to lead to a reversion to the wild-type phenotype.
Option B: Addition of another normal gene
• This would introduce a normal copy of the gene into the genome, which could potentially compensate for the mutant gene.
This process is often referred to as gene therapy. • While this can restore the wild-type phenotype, it is not a mutation but rather an addition of genetic material.
Therefore, It does not fit the question's criteria of a mutation leading to reversion.
Option C: Deletion of the mutated gene
Deleting a mutant gene would remove its effects but would not necessarily result in a wild-type phenotype unless there is a compensatory mechanism in place.
• Deletion does not count as a mutation that causes reversion to the wild-type phenotype.

stem: A mother was concerned about the health of her newborn baby as her previous child had Phenylketonuria. Which test should be done for detecting metabolite levels in Inborn errors of metabolism in neonates?
subject: Pediatrics, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Tandem mass spectroscopy (MS/MS) is widely used for newborn screening for metabolic disorders.
It allows for the detection of multiple metabolic diseases from a single blood sample.
It is highly sensitive and can identify various metabolic abnormalities by analyzing specific metabolites and their precursors.

Other Options:
Option B: Complete blood count (CBC): CBC is a common blood test used to evaluate overall health and detect a wide range of disorders, including anemia, infection, and many other diseases.
However, it is not specifically designed for metabolic disease screening.
Option C: Urinalysis: Useful for evaluating kidney function and urinary tract issues but not comprehensive enough for metabolic disease screening.
Option D: Genetic testing: Identifies specific genetic mutations, often used when there is a high suspicion of a particular disorder, but not a first-line screening tool due to cost and specificity.

stem: Which of the following neurotransmitter is not derived from amino acid?
subject: Biochemistry, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
Creatinine is a product of glycine and arginine, which is an amino acid, but creatinine is not a neurotransmitter.

Other Options:
Option A: GABA (Gamma-Aminobutyric Acid): GABA is a neurotransmitter that is made from the amino acid glutamate through a decarboxylation reaction catalyzed by the enzyme glutamate decarboxylase.
Option C: Dopamine: Dopamine is a neurotransmitter produced from phenylalanine (amino acid) through decarboxylation.
Option D: 5 hydroxytryptamine: Serotonin (5-hydroxytryptamine) is a neurotransmitter synthesized from the amino acid tryptophan.

stem: Which of the following is not a product of heme breakdown?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
Aminolevulinic acid is not a product of heme breakdown.
It is an intermediate in the biosynthesis of heme, not a breakdown product.

Other Options:
Option A: Biliverdin: Biliverdin is a direct product of heme breakdown.
Heme oxygenase catalyzes the conversion of heme to biliverdin.
Option B: Carbon monoxide: Carbon monoxide is also a product of the heme breakdown process.
It is released when heme is converted to biliverdin by heme oxygenase.
Option C: Ferric lon: Ferric lon (Fe3+) is released during the breakdown of heme.
Heme oxygenase cleaves the heme ring to release Iron in its ferric form.

stem: FISH technique helps to identify all of the following except?
subject: Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• FISH (Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization) is not useful for detecting point mutations.
• FISH is designed to detect larger genetic changes such as deletions, duplications, translocations, and amplifications.
• Point mutations involve a single nucleotide change, which is too small for FISH to detect.

Other Options:
Option B: Translocation: FISH is useful for detecting translocations because it can identify specific chromosome rearrangements by using probes that bind to the translocated segments.
Option C: Deletion: FISH can detect deletions by using probes that bind to the region where the deletion occurs.
If the probe does not bind, it indicates a deletion in that area.
Option D: Gene Amplification: FISH can detect gene amplifications by showing an increased number of signals from the probe, indicating that there are multiple copies of a particular gene or chromosomal region.

stem:
Which of the following is not a Features of Korsak off Psychosis?
1. Amnesia
2. Confabulation
3. Ophthalmoplegia
4. Polyneuropathy.
subject: Psychiatry, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
1. Amnesia: This is a key feature of Korsakoff Psychosis, characterized by significant memory loss, particularly anterograde amnesia (inability to form new memories).
2. Confabulation: This is another key feature of Korsakoff Psychosis, where the patient fabricates stories to fill in memory gaps, often without intending to deceive.
3. Ophthalmoplegia: This is primarily a feature of Wernicke's encephalopathy, not Korsakoff Psychosis.
4. Polyneuropathy: This can be associated with chronic alcoholism and thiamine deficiency but is not a primary feature of Korsakoff Psychosis itself.

Other Options:
Option A, B, & C: Refer to explanation of Option D.

stem: Which gene defect causes familial hypercholesterolemia?
subject: Biochemistry, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Familial hypercholesterolemia is most commonly caused by mutations in the gene coding for the LDL receptor.
• This receptor is responsible for the uptake of LDL cholesterol from the bloodstream into the liver.
• Defects in this receptor lead to high levels of LDL cholesterol in the blood.

Other Options:
Option A: Apo E: Mutations in the Apo E gene are associated with different lipid disorders, such as familial dysbetalipoproteinemia, but not typically famillal hypercholesterolemia.
Option B: Apo C II: Apo C II is a cofactor for lipoprotein lipase, and defects in this gene are linked to hypertriglyceridemia, not hypercholesterolemia.
Option D: Apo B48: Apo B48 is a form of apolipoprotein B that is involved in chylomicron formation and is not related to familial hypercholesterolemia.
The defect in familial hypercholesterolemia involves Apo 8100, not Apo B48.

stem: A female patient presented to OPD with Tanner stage 4 development and also complaints about hyperpigmentation. Her blood pressure was noted 146/96 and pulse rate of 105 BPM. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?
subject: Pediatrics, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• This enzyme deficiency leads to an accumulation of 11-deoxycorticosterone (DOC), which has mineralocorticoid activity, causing hypertension.
Patients with 11-beta hydroxylase deficiency typically present with signs of virilization (such as Tanner stage 4) and hyperpigmentation due to increased ACTH stimulation,

Other Options:
Option A: 21 beta hydroxylase deficiency: This enzyme deficiency typically causes virilization and hyperpigmentation but is associated with hypotension, not hypertension.
Option C: 17 beta hydroxylase deficiency: This enzyme deficiency usually presents with hypertension and ambiguous genitalia in males or lack of secondary sexual characteristics in females, but not virilization (Tanner stage 4).
Option D: 17 alpha hydroxylase deficiency: This enzyme deficiency leads to hypertension and sexual infantilism, but not virilization or hyperpigmentation.

stem: In the given enzyme kinetics graphs, Identify the types of inhibitors labeled as A and B.
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
Competitive Inhibition (Inhibitor A):
• In a Lineweaver-Burk plot, competitive inhibition is characterized by lines that intersect on the y-axis.
• This is because the presence of a competitive inhibitor increases the apparent Km (Michaelis constant), but the Vmax (maximum velocity) remains unchanged.
The inhibitor competes with the substrate for the active site of the enzyme.
Non-Competitive Inhibition (Inhibitor B):
In a Lineweaver-Burk plot, non-competitive Inhibition is shown by lines that intersect on the x-axis.
This occurs because non-competitive inhibitors decrease the Vmax but do not affect the Km.
The Inhibitor binds to an allosteric site (not the active site), affecting the enzyme's function regardless of the substrate concentration.

Other Options:
Option B: Uncompetitive and Non-Competitive: Uncompetitive inhibition shows parallel lines in a Lineweaver-Burk plot, as both Vmax and Km are reduced proportionally.
This does not match the provided data for Inhibitor A.
Option C: Non-Competitive and Un-competitive: This option incorrectly identifies the inhibitors in reverse order.
The data provided for Inhibitor A matches competitive inhibition, not uncompetitive.
Option D: Non-competitive and competitive: Like option C, this option reverses the identification of Inhibitors A and B.

stem: A 35 year old patient presented to medicine OPD for generalized fatigue, headache and weakness. Which of the following statements is not true regarding folate deficiency?
options: Folate supplementation in pregnancy prevents NTD
High Homocysteine and Normal Methylmalonic acid
Treat coexisting B12 deficiency before initiating folate therapy
subject: Medicine, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Hemolytic anemia is not a feature of folate deficiency.
• Folate deficiency typically causes megaloblastic anemia, characterized by the presence of large, immature red blood cells.
• Hemolytic anemia Involves the destruction of red blood cells and is not directly related to folate deficiency.

Other Options:
Option A: Folate supplementation in pregnancy prevents NTD: Folate, also known as folic acid when it's in its synthetic form, is crucial during pregnancy for preventing neural tube defects (NTDs) in the developing fetus.
Option B: High Homocysteine and Normal Methylmalonic Acid:
In folate deficiency, homocysteine levels are elevated because folate is necessary for its conversion to methionine.
• However, methylmalonic acid levels remain normal because MMA accumulation is specifically associated with vitamin B12 deficiency.
Option D: Treat coexisting B12 deficiency before Initiating folate therapy:
B12 deficiency indirectly causes folate deficiency.
It's important to treat coexisting B12 deficiency before initiating folate therapy.
• Folate supplementation can improve the production of red blood cells, which may mask the anemia symptoms of B12 deficiency.
This can lead to a delay in diagnosing and treating the underlying B12 deficiency, which, if left untreated, can cause Irreversible nerve damage.

stem: A 50 year old diabetic male presented to a routine kidney checkup for diabetic kidney disease. Which of the following can lead to a decrease in his GFR?
subject: Physiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Net filtration pressure = PC-PB+πB-πC
PC-Hydrostatic pressure of glomerular capillaries
PB-Hydrostatic pressure of Bowman's capsule
- Oncotic pressure of Bowman's capsule
πC-Oncotic pressure of glomerular capillaries
Hence, a decrease in Bowman's oncotic pressure causes a decrease in GFR rather than an increase.

Other Options:
Option A: Increased RBF: Increased blood flow to the kidney enhances blood delivery to the glomerulus, facilitating filtration, while decreased flow hampers filtration.
Option B: Increased Glomerular hydrostatic pressure: An Increase in Glomerular hydrostatic pressure increases the net forces, hence increasing the GFR.
Option D: Decreased Glomerular oncotic pressure: Glomerular capillary osmotic pressure, from plasma proteins, opposes filtration.
So, low protein levels Increase GFR, while dehydration reduces GFR and urine production.

stem:
Which of the following are properties of left ventricular cardiac muscle?
1. All or none law
2. Length tension relationship
3. Long refractory period
4. Can be tetanized
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
Correct statements:
Statement 1: All or None Law
This is a fundamental property of ventricular cardiac muscle, which states that once a threshold is reached, the cardiac muscle fibers contract maximally.
There is no partial contraction in response to a stimulus; the response is either full or none.
Statement 2: Length-Tension Relationship
Ventricular cardiac muscle follows the length-tension relationship, where the force of contraction is dependent on the initial length of the cardiac muscle fibers.
This relationship is crucial for the heart's function, allowing it to pump efficiently by adjusting the force of contraction based on the volume of blood filling the heart (preload).
Statement 3: Long Refractory Perlod
Cardiac muscle cells have a long refractory period, which is significantly longer than that of skeletal muscle cells.
This long refractory period is essential to prevent tetanization (sustained contraction) and to ensure proper rhythmic contractions of the heart.

Other Options:
Incorrect statements
Statement 4: Can be Tetanized
Unlike skeletal muscle, ventricular cardiac muscle cannot be tetanized due to its long refractory period.
This characteristic is vital to maintaining the rhythmic and cyclical nature of heartbeats, preventing continuous contraction that would impede normal heart function.

stem: Spinal nucleus of trigeminal is associated with which sensation?
subject: Anatomy, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
The spinal nucleus of the trigeminal nerve is primarily associated with the sensation of pain and temperature.

Other Options:
Option A: Vibration: Vibration sensation is primarily transmitted through the dorsal column-medial lemniscus pathway, not the spinal nucleus of the trigeminal nerve.
Option B: Conscious proprioception: Conscious proprioception is also transmitted through the dorsal column-medial lemniscus pathway, not the trigeminal nerve.
Option D: Two-point discrimination: Two-point discrimination is mediated by the dorsal column-medial lemniscus pathway and the dorsal root ganglia, not the trigeminal nerve,

stem:
Erythropoietin is secreted by which of the following organs?
A. Liver
B. Kidney
C. Muscle
D. Heart
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
Erythropoietin (EPO) is produced from peritubular epithelium cells of the kidney and perivenous hepatocytes of the liver.

Other Options:
Option B, C & D are incorrect.

stem:
Which of the following symptoms are typically associated with Lower Motor Neuron (LMN) lesions?
1. Atrophy
2. Fasciculations
3. DTR increased
4. Rigidity
subject: Medicine, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• When lower motor neurons are disrupted, the muscle loses its innervation, resulting in significant muscle atrophy due to the cessation of nerve growth factor secretion and loss of muscle function.
• Denervation potentials such as fibrillation or fascicular twitches are observed in EMG recordings as a result of muscle denervation.

Other Options:
Options B, C & D are incorrect.

stem: Which hormone does not play a significant role in in-utero growth of a fetus?
subject: Physiology, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
Despite being present in high levels in the fetus, growth hormone is not recognized for significantly influencing fetal growth.

Other Options:
Options A, B & D: Insulin, thyroid hormone, and cortisol play Important roles in the in utero growth of a fetus.

subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
The Image represents the feedback regulation of GH secretion. The hypothalamus releases GHRH to stimulate growth hormone (GH) secretion and somatostatin to inhibit it.
• GH prompts the production of insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1), which then negatively influences GH secretion.
IGF-1 acts at both pituitary and hypothalamus levels.

Other Options:
Options B, C & D are incorrect.

stem: In response to Increased blood pressure, which of the following is released at the level of the SA node?
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
When there is an increase in blood pressure it stimulates the baroreceptor reflex which then stimulates the nucleus tractus solitarius (NTS) which in turn stimulates the Cardiac Inhibitory center (CIC), the center for parasympathetic activity.
This causes the release of Acetylcholine at the level of nodal tissue.

Other Options:
Options A, B & C are incorrect.

stem: A 35 year old male presented to dermatology OPD for skin condition after starting his new medication. Which among the following anti-leprosy drugs can cause skin hyperpigmentation?
subject: Pharmacology, Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
• Clofazimine is a fat-soluble riminophenazine dye used in the treatment of leprosy.
Prolonged treatment with clofazimine leads to extensive bioaccumulation and precipitation throughout the body, forming crystal-like drug inclusions (CLDIs).
Common side effects of the drug include gastrointestinal problems, ichthyosis, and discoloration of skin, eyes, and bodily secretions.
This discoloration, which can lead to skin hyperpigmentation, is one of the more distressing or stigmatizing effects of the drug.
It can also prolong the QT interval on an ECG.

Other Options:
Options B, C, & D are not typically associated with discoloration of skin or hyperpigmentation.

stem: Which among the following is an HER-1 Inhibitor?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
Panitumumab is a human IgG2k monoclonal antibody that binds to the extracellular domain III of the epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR or HER-1).
• By binding to this receptor, panitumumab prevents ligand-dependent signaling, effectively Inhibiting the EGFR pathway. which is often upregulated in certain types of cancer.

Other Options:
Options A, B, and D are not inhibitors of EGFR or HER-1. They inhibit tyrosine kinase receptors.
Option B: Crizotinib: Crizotinib is an orally bloavailable inhibitor of several receptor tyrosine kinases, Including ROS1, HGFR/MET, and ALK.
It is primarily used for non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) that is ALK-positive or ROS1-positive.
Option C: Alectinib: Alectinib is also an orally bioavailable inhibitor of ALK kinase activity.
It shows a higher selectivity for ALK than crizotinib and is active against mutant forms of ALK found in tumors resistant to crizotinib.
Option D: Lorlatinib: Loriatinib is a third-generation ALK Inhibitor that targets ALK and ROS1.

stem: Which of the following is Pcsk9 inhibitor?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Evolocumab is used for lowering LDL cholesterol in patients with hypercholesterolemia.
It is a monoclonal antibody that inhibits Proprotein Convertase Subtilisin/Kexin Type 9 (PCSK9).
By binding to PCSK9, it prevents PCSK9 from degrading the low-density lipoprotein (LDL) receptors on hepatocytes.
This increases the number of LDL receptors available to clear LDL cholesterol from the blood, thus lowering LDL cholesterol levels.

Other Options:
Option B: Lomitapide: Lomitapide inhibits the microsomal triglyceride transfer protein (MTP), which is essential for the assembly and secretion of very low-density lipoprotein (VLDL) In the liver.
This reduces the levels of LDL cholesterol and triglycerides.
Option C: Mipomersen: Mipomersen is an antisense oligonucleotide that targets and inhibits the synthesis of apolipoprotein B- 100 (apoB-100), the primary protein component of LDL and VLDL.
This reduces LDL cholesterol.
Option D: Torcetrapib: Torcetrapib was a cholesteryl ester transfer protein (CETP) Inhibitor Intended to increase high-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol.
However, its development was terminated due to adverse effects in clinical trials, including Increased mortality.

stem:
Choose the correct option with diuretics and it's primary site of action:
1. Osmotic diuretics - Distal convoluted tubule..
2. Thiazide diuretics - Proximal convoluted tubule
3. Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors - Proximal convoluted tubule
4. Potassium-sparing diuretics-Collecting duct.
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
Osmotic diuretics: Proximal convoluted tubule and descending limb of the loop of Henle, not the distal convoluted tubule.
• Thiazide diuretics: It mainly acts on the distal convoluted tubule the proximal convoluted tubule may be a secondary site of action.
• Carbonic anhydrase Inhibitors: Proximal convoluted tubule.
• Potassium-sparing diuretics: Collecting duct..

Other Options:
Options A, C, & D are incorrect.

stem: A 46 year old female with paget's disease presented to a clinic for teriparatide treatment. Which of the following adverse effects prevents the use of Teriparatide in this patient?
subject: Pharmacology, Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
• Teriparatide is contraindicated in patients with Paget's disease due to the increased baseline risk of osteosarcoma associated with these patients.
Although postmarketing surveillance has not established a definitive causal association between teriparatide use and osteosarcoma, It remains a serious concern due to findings in animal studies.
Therefore, teriparatide carries a black-box warning for the potential risk of osteosarcoma.
• As a result, its use is limited to a maximum of 2 years and should be avoided in patients with conditions that increase the baseline risk for osteosarcoma, such as Paget disease of bone, unexplained elevations of alkaline phosphatase, open epiphyses, or prior radiation therapy involving the skeleton.

Other Options:
Options A, C, & D are not recognized adverse effects of Teriparatide.

stem: A 30 year old male patient was prescribed anticoagulants by a physician. Which among the following anticoagulants requires aPTT monitoring?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Lepirudin is a recombinant form of hirudin. Desirudin and lepirudin are medications used to treat thrombosis in patients with heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
• They are eliminated by the kidneys, with a half-life of about 2 hours after subcutaneous administration and approximately 10 minutes after intravenous Infusion.
Caution should be exercised when using these drugs in patients with decreased renal function, and dally monitoring of serum creatinine and aPTT levels is recommended.

Other Options:
Option A: Apixaban
This is an oral direct factor Xa inhibitor. Apixaban is given in fixed doses and does not require routine coagulation monitoring.
The drugs affect the PT more than the aPTT, but the PT does not reliably measure their anticoagulant activity.
• Anti-factor Xa assays can measure drug levels. it's important to assess renal function at least yearly in patients taking oral factor Xa inhibitors or more frequently in patients with renal dysfunction.
Option B: Enoxaparin
• This is a low molecular weight heparin (LMWH). LMWH usually produces a predictable anticoagulant response, so routine monitoring is not needed.
• However, patients with renal Impairment, obesity, pregnancy, and children may require monitoring using an anti-factor Xa assay due to potential variations in the drug's effects.
Option C: Dabigatran
• While dabigatran prolongs the aPTT, routine monitoring is not required due to its predictable anticoagulant response at fixed doses.
Thrombin time is too sensitive for monitoring dabigatran, and a diluted thrombin time assay has been developed for quantifying plasma dabigatran concentrations, but these are not routinely used.

stem: Sugammadex is used as a reversal agent for which of the following NMDA?
subject: Pharmacology, Anaesthesia
explanation:
Solution
Sugammadex is a modified cyclodextrin that, when administered intravenously, specifically binds to aminosteroidal muscle relaxants.
• Aminosteroidal muscle relaxants include: Pancuronium, vecuronium, rocuronium, rapacuronium.

Other Options:
Option B: (Mivacurium) & C (Atracurlum) are Benzylisoquinolinium compounds.
Option D: Scoline (Succinylcholine) is a depolarizing NMBA and is not reversed by Sugammadex.

stem: A chronic alcoholic patient was admitted to ward for hepatic pathology. After 3-4 days, he developed whole-body tremors along with vomiting and visual and auditory hallucinations. Which among the following is used to prevent another episode of these symptoms?
subject: Pharmacology, Psychiatry, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• This medication belongs to the benzodiazepine class and is commonly used to manage alcohol withdrawal symptoms, Including tremors, anxiety, agitation, and seizures.
It acts by enhancing the inhibitory effects of gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) in the brain, thereby reducing the excitability of neurons and calming the central nervous system.
It is long-acting and self-tapering because of active metabolites.

Other Options:
Option B: Clonidine: While clonidine can be used to manage some symptoms of alcohol withdrawal, such as hypertension and agitation, it is not typically used as a first-line agent for preventing the full spectrum of withdrawal symptoms, including tremors, vomiting, and hallucinations.
Option C: Topiramate: Topiramate is an antiepileptic drug that has been studied for its potential role in reducing alcohol cravings and promoting abstinence in patients with alcohol dependence. However, it is not primarily used to manage acute alcohol withdrawal symptoms.
Option D: Antabuse (Disulfiram): Disulfiram is a medication used to support the treatment of chronic alcoholism by producing an acute sensitivity to alcohol. It works by inhibiting the enzyme acetaldehyde dehydrogenase, leading to an accumulation of acetaldehyde in the body when alcohol is consumed. However, it is not typically used to manage acute alcohol withdrawal symptoms.

stem: A 37 year old male was prescribed anti diabetic drugs a few months ago. He is currently complaining of diarrhea and some weight loss also. Which of the following antidiabetic drugs can cause unexplained diarrhea?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The most common side effects (10%-25%) of metformin are gastrointestinal: nausea, indigestion, abdominal cramps or bloating, diarrhea, or some combination of these.
Chronic treatment with metformin decreases bile acid absorption. The Increased concentration of bile salts in the Intestines, possibly caused by reduced absorption, may lead to diarrhea, which is often associated with metformin treatment.

Other Options:
Option B: Glimepiride: Glimepiride, a sulfonylurea, is not commonly associated with diarrhea as a primary side effect. It can cause hypoglycemia and weight gain.
Option C: Pioglitazone: Pioglitazone can cause weight gain and fluid retention.
Option D: Sitagliptin: Sitagliptin, a DPP-4 inhibitor, is generally well-tolerated and is not commonly associated with unexplained diarrhea.

stem: Which anti-diabetic drugs are approved for treatment in heart failure?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Dapagliflozin was the first SGLT2 inhibitor approved for treating heart failure in the U.S. in 2020. Its main effects include Improved diuresis and preload reduction by increasing glucose excretion in the urine and sodium and water.
It may also have off-target effects that improve cardiac function.

Other Options:
Options B, C, & D are primarily used for glycemic control alone. While GLP-1 agonists have shown some cardiovascular benefits in clinical trials, they are not approved for the treatment in heart failure.

stem: Which of the following is false about voriconazole?
options: It should be taken on an empty stomach
It should not be co-administered with pyrazinamide
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
There is no known significant interaction between voriconazole and pyrazinamide.
Voriconazole's interactions primarily involve drugs metabolized by CYP2C19, CYP2C9, and CYP3A4.

Other Options:
Option A: It should be taken on an empty stomach: Because high-fat meals can reduce voriconazole bioavailability, the oral drug should be administered either 1 hour before or 1 hour after meals.
Option B: It requires therapeutic drug monitoring: Therapeutic drug monitoring is vital for maintaining target serum concentrations between 1 and 5 mg/L, particularly for children due to their unpredictable plasma levels of voriconazole.
Option D: It requires a loading dose: Voriconazole typically requires a loading dose to quickly reach therapeutic levels.

stem:
Which of the following acts by suicide inhibition?
1. Spironolactone
2. Ethinyl estradiol
3. Benzbromarone
4. Ritonavir
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Suicide Inhibition is a type of enzyme inhibition in which the inhibitor forms a covalent bond with the enzyme, essentially inactivating it irreversibly.
1. Spironolactone: Drugs such as spironolactone, eplerenone, and finerenone competitively Inhibit the binding of aldosterone to the mineralocorticoid receptors.
2. Ethinyl estradiol: Ethinyl estradiol is a synthetic estrogen used in contraceptive pills. It does not act by suicide inhibition.
3. Benzbromarone: Benzbromarone is a uricosuric agent used to treat gout. It inhibits xanthine oxidase irreversibly, making It a suicide inhibitor.
4. Ritonavir: Ritonavir is a protease inhibitor used to treat HIV/AIDS. It inhibits the HIV protease enzyme Irreversibly, making It a suicide inhibitor.

Other Options:
Options A, B, & D are incorrect.

stem: A 25 year old patient presents with a cough and thick sputum. What should be the drug given to this patient?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
This drug is a mucolytic agent that helps to thin and loosen mucus in the airways, making it easier to cough up and clear from the respiratory tract.
It is particularly useful for patients with thick sputum.

Other Options:
Options B, C, & D are cough suppressants and are not recommended In case of thick sputum.

stem: A 38 year old female patient with type 2 diabetes has shown microalbuminuria in two separate urine analyses six months apart, with normal renal function test results. Which of the following is not useful in preventing renal damage progression?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
This is a thiazide diuretic commonly used to treat hypertension.
While it helps control blood pressure, it is not specifically beneficial for preventing diabetic nephropathy progression.

Other Options:
Option A: Canagliflozin: This is a sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 (SGLT2) inhibitor. SGLT2 inhibitors have been shown to reduce the progression of diabetic nephropathy and lower the risk of renal events in patients with type 2 diabetes.
Option B: Finerenone: This is a non-steroidal mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (MRA). It has been shown to have renal- protective effects in patients with diabetic nephropathy by reducing albuminuria and slowing the progression of kidney disease.
Option D: Dapagliflozin: This is another SGLT2 inhibitor with similar renal-protective benefits as canagliflozin.

stem: A G2P1 delivered a preterm baby at 36 weeks. The baby was admitted to the NICU and given expressed breast milk. Unfortunately, the baby did not survive. What is used to reduce pain and engorgement of the lactating breast in this case?
subject: Pharmacology, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Cabergoline is a potent agonist at D2 receptors and has a lower affinity for D1, 5HT, and a-adrenergic receptors.
It inhibits prolactin secretion, thereby suppressing lactation. It is commonly used in situations where lactation needs to be suppressed, such as in cases of fetal loss or when breastfeeding is not desired or feasible.

Other Options:
Option A: Pramipexole: Pramipexole and ropinirole are widely used in the treatment of Parkinson's disease. They are agonists at all D2-like receptors but have the highest affinities for the D3 receptor subtype. However, these agents are less effective than L-DOPA in the early stages of PD treatment.
Option B: Chlorpromazine: This antipsychotic medication can elevate prolactin levels, which contraindicates its use in this case.
Option C: Sumatriptan: This is a medication used to treat migraines. It does not have any effect on prolactin secretion or lactation.

stem: A 38 year old patient was recently diagnosed with type 2 diabetes. Which of the following drugs increases the risk of UTI in this patient?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
This is an SGLT2 inhibitor used to manage type 2 diabetes.
SGLT2 inhibitors increase the amount of glucose excreted in the urine, which can create an environment conducive to bacterial growth, thereby increasing the risk of UTIs and genital infections.

Other Options:
Options A, C, & D do not generally increase the risk of UTI.

stem: Which drug is preferred for a 55-year-old with 150/100 mm Hg BP and HbA1c 7.8%?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Given the cardiovascular benefits demonstrated by GLP-1 receptor agonists like liraglutide, dulaglutide, and semaglutide, the most suitable option from the provided list would be Liraglutide.
• GLP-1 receptor agonists have shown superiority over placebo in reducing the risk of cardiovascular events such as death from cardiovascular disease, stroke, or myocardial Infarction.
These medications provide additional cardiovascular protection beyond their glucose-lowering effects, making them particularly beneficial for patients with diabetes who have a high risk of cardiovascular events.

Other Options:
Options A, B, & C, have not shown significant cardiovascular benefits as Liraglutide.

stem:
Match the following?
Column AColumn B
1. Patch testA. Leprosy
2. KOH mountB. Lupus Vulgaris
3. BiopsyC. Contact dermatitis
4. Slit skin smearD. Tinea
subject: Dermatology, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Patch test is done to detect the hypersensitivity with allergens in contact dermatitis.
• KOH mount is done to detect Tinea Infections which show a presence of branching hyaline septate hyphae.
• Biopsy is the preferred Investigation in case of Lupus Vulgaris.
• Slit skin smear is used in the diagnosis of Leprosy by taking skin samples over the ear lobes.

Other Options:
Option B, C, & D:
These options are incorrect.

stem:
Match the following viruses with their corresponding nucleic acid type:
VirusNucleic Acid Type
1. RotavirusA. ssRNA
2. SARS-CoV-2 (COVID-19 virus)B. dsRNA
3. ParvovirusC. dsDNA
4. Human papillomavirus (HPV)D. ssDNA
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
VirusNucleic Acid Type
1. RotavirusB. dsRNA
2. SARS-CoV-2 (COVID-19 virus)A. ssRNA
3. ParvovirusD. ssDNA
4. Human papillomavirus (HPV)C. dsDNA


Other Options:
Option A, C & D:
These options are incorrect options.

stem: Select the correct statement regarding the organism shown in the Image:
options: Patient is at high risk with CD4<900
Lung is the primary site of infection
subject: Microbiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
The above Image shows capsulated, budding yeast cells in the India-Ink stain which is characteristic of Cryptococcus neoformans It is a fungus abundant particularly in the feces of pigeons and other bird droppings.
The Infection is usually acquired by Inhalation leading to mild pneumonitis, but it affects the brain more, causing meningitis.

Other Options:
Option A: Humans are the only reservoir: Cryptococcus is found in the feces of pigeons and bird droppings. Hence this statement is false.
Option B: Patient is at high risk with CD4<900: Patients with a CD4 <200 are at the risk of Infection.
Option D: Pseudohyphae and budding is seen: Pseudohyphae and budding is seen in Candida species.

stem: A pregnant woman presents with a history of painful genital ulcers during pregnancy. Her newborn also has similar ulcers. Examination of a smear reveals the presence of Tzanck cells. What is the likely diagnosis?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Genital ulcer with a child affected with similar lesions points towards diagnosis of HSV2.
HSV2 is transmitted vertically and sexually, affecting below the waist.
Clinical features include genital lesions below the waist and neonatal herpes (mother-to-child transmission).
Other features Include: Gingivostomatitis, Encephalitis, Meningitis, and Dendritic ulcers. Tzanck smear shows multinucleated glant cells with faceted nuclel and homogeneously stained 'ground glass chromatin (Tzanck cells) constituting a positive smear.

Other Options:
Option B: VZV: VZV Is a virus similar in morphology to HSV but it causes chicken pox which presents with skin lesions, not ulcers.
Option C: HPV: Human papilloma virus causes plantar wart, Epidermodysplasia, Laryngeal papillomatosis, Condyloma acuminata.
Option D: Klebsiella: Klebsiella is a gram-negative bacteria which is a common cause of community-acquired pneumonia.

stem: A 28 year old male patient presents with fever, fatigue and retro-orbital pain after 5 days, suggestive of dengue hemorrhagic fever. Which investigation is appropriate for confirmation?
subject: Medicine, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
IgM ELISA test offers a reliable diagnosis, usually positive after the 5th day.

Other Options:
Option A: NS-1 antigen test:
• The mainstay of diagnosis is the detection of a non-structural viral protein antigen (NS-1).
• This can be detected in the blood for up to 1-4 days.
Option B: Viral culture: Viral culture is rarely done in detection of the dengue virus.
Option C: Circulating IgM: Circulating IgM is detected from day 7.

stem: Assertion: Myocarditis is a common complication of faucial diphtheria. Reason: It is due to bacteremia by diphtheria.
options: Assertion is true, reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.
Assertion is false, reason is false
Assertion is true and reason is false
Assertion is false and reason is true
subject: Medicine, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
Toxemla is the reason for myocarditis caused by faucial diphtheria, The bacilli remain confined to the site of entry, where they multiply and form the toxin, which is absorbed and produces toxic damage to the heart (myocarditis), kidney (tubular necrosis), liver, and adrenal glands.

Other Options:
Option A, B, & D:
These options are incorrect.

stem: Which of the following parasites demonstrates parthenogenesis?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Parthenogenesis is the ability of the females to reproduce asexually.
• Strongyloides stercoralis is the smallest nematode that is capable of parthenogenesis.
• Egg is not seen in Strongyloides stercoralis-Ovoviviparous.

Other Options:
Option A, B, & D:
These options are incorrect as these parasites are not capable of parthenogenesis.

stem: What is the best investigation for Identifying malaria species?
options: Acridine orange stain with thin smear
subject: Microbiology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
Thin smear with Giemsa stain under light microscopy is the gold standard in the identification of malarial species.
Sensitivity: 200 parasites/microlitre. Jaswant Singh and Bhattacharya staining is preferred as per the National malaria program.

Other Options:
Option A: Quantitative buffy coat: Quantitative buffy coat is the microhematocrit method of diagnosing malaria. But it is not the best test for malaria speciation identification.
Option C: Thick smear with Glemsa staln: Thick smear with Giemsa stain is the gold standard for quantification. Sensitivity is 5 parasites/microlitre.
Option D: Acridine orange stain with thin smear: Acridine orange stain with thin smear is expensive and not advised as per the National malaria program.

stem: An HIV patient presented with watery diarrhea. Stool examination revealed oocysts of size 20-30 microns and oval-shaped organisms, as shown in the image below. Which among the following is the correct answer?
subject: Microbiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Cystoisospora belli are long and oval-shaped organisms, measuring 20-33 microns in size.
These organisms affect immunosuppressed patients, particularly those with AIDS, often presenting with profuse diarrhea associated with weakness, anorexia, and weight loss.

Other Options:
Option A: Cryptosporidium: Cryptosporidium is 4-6 microns in size and round in shape.
Option C: Cyclospora: Cyclospora is 8-12 microns in size and round in shape.
Option D: Microsporidia: Microsporidia is 1-4 microns in size and possesses a polar tubule.

stem: Assertion: P. falciparum doesn't show schizont stage in peripheral blood. Reason: They get sequestered in small capillaries due to attachment to membranes.
options: Both the assertion and reason are correct, and the reason explains the assertion.
Both the assertion and reason are correct, but the reason does not explain the assertion.
The assertion is correct, but the reason is incorrect.
The assertion is Incorrect, but the reason is correct.
subject: Microbiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Schizonts of P. falciparum are not seen in the peripheral blood, they are seen only in the brain vasculature.
This is due to the cytoadherence property of P. falciparum.
Red blood cell -> Infected by P. falciparum (infects brain) -> P. falciparum starts making knobs - PF-Erythrocyte Membrane Protein -> These knobs will bind to ICAM present in endothelial cells -> The cell will get adhered - Cytoadherence

Other Options:
Option B, C, & D:
These options are Incorrect.

stem: A patient with a history of penicillin allergy has been diagnosed with Enterococcus infective endocarditis. Which of the following antibiotics is the most appropriate treatment option?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• In Enterococcal infective endocarditis patients with penicillin allergy or penicillin-resistant strains, the drug of choice in the treatment of such cases is vancomycin. Enterococci are intrinsically resistant to a wide array of antimicrobial agents, and they are generally resistant to killing by any of the single agents (e.g., ampicillin or vancomycin) that are bactericidal for most other gram-positive coccl.
Effective bactericidal activity can only be achieved with the combination of a cell wall-active agent, such as ampicillin or vancomycin, and an aminoglycoside, such as gentamicin or streptomycin.

Other Options:
Option B: Ceftriaxone: Ceftriaxone is a 3rd generation cephalosporin used in the treatment of community-acquired gram- negative Infections.
Option C: Piperacillin: Piperacillin is frequently used in treating gram-negative infections in neutropenic/Immunocompromised or burn patients.
Option D: Aztreonam: Aztreonam is a beta-lactam antibiotic that Inhibits enteric bacilli, H. Influenzae, and pseudomonas.

stem: In which of the following bacteria is increased penicillin drug resistance is noted?
subject: Microbiology, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• The Gonococcal Isolate Surveillance Project (GISP) is a national collaborative surveillance project in the United States.
The program was established in 1986 to analyze and monitor antimicrobial resistance trends among N. gonorrhoeae.
• Meningococci were uniformly sensitive to penicillin and other antibiotics, but resistant strains have emerged and become common in many areas.
This penicillin resistance is due to the ability of the Neisseria to produce penicillinase.
• They are also resistant to fluoroquinolones.

Other Options:
Option A, C, & D: Penicillin resistance is not noted in these diseases.

stem:
Disposable pipette tips are sterilized by which of the following methods?
A. Autoclave
B. Hot air oven
C. Microwave
D. 70% ethanol
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Disposable pipettes are sterilized by autoclave.
• All plastic and rubber materials are sterilized by autoclave.

Other Options:
Option A, B, & C: These are incorrect options:
• Hot air oven: Sharps, powdery, and olly materials are sterilized by hot air oven.
• Microwave: Another useful method of sterilization of small-volume waste at the point of generation, but ideally not used for animal or human body parts, metal items, or toxic or radioactive material.
• 70% ethanol: It is used in disinfection and it is a low-level disinfectant.

stem: Parvo B19 is associated with all except?
options: Transient aplastic crisis with hemolytic anemia
subject: Microbiology, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
Persistent pure red cell aplasia in immunocompromised is associated with Parvo B-19 virus, not aplastic anemia.

Other Options:
Option A: Fever with petechial rash: Parvo B19 virus is associated with Erythema Infectiosum, which presents with a macular erythematous rash present on the cheek.
Option B: Transient aplastic crisis with hemolytic anemia: Transient aplastic crisis with hemolytic anemla is associated with Parvo B-19 virus.
Option D: Hydrops fetalls: Hydrops fetalis is associated with Parvo B-19 virus.

stem: Which among the following is the most specific disease of Streptococcus agalactiae?
subject: Microbiology, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Streptococcus agalactiae is a group B B-hemolytic streptococci which is the single most common cause of neonatal meningitis.
Early onset type - Occurs within a week of birth.
Late onset type - Develops between the second and twelfth weeks of life.
The more common early onset type presents as meningitis or septicemia and is often fatal.

Other Options:
Option B: Infective endocarditis: Infective endocarditis is caused by Staph, aureus, Staph, epidermidis, Streptococcus viridans.
Option C: Dental caries: Dental caries is caused by other species of streptococcus like Streptococcus mitis.
Option D: Pharyngitis with or without tonsillitis: Pharyngitis with or without tonsillitis is caused by Group A streptococcus.

stem: A patient came to the OPD with an injury caused by a rusted metal. What is the mechanism of action of Tetanus toxin?
options: Inhibit the release of GABA and Glycine
subject: Microbiology, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Tetanospasmin is the toxin responsible for tetanus.
• Tetanospasmin resembles strychnine in its effects. The tetanus toxin specifically blocks synaptic inhibition in the spinal cord, presumably at inhibitory terminals that use glycine and GABA as neurotransmitters.
The abolition of spinal inhibition causes uncontrolled spread of Impulses initiated anywhere in the central nervous system.
This results in muscle rigidity and spasms due to the simultaneous contraction of agonists and antagonists, in the absence of reciprocal inhibition.

Other Options:
Option A: Inhibition of protein synthesis: Shiga toxin or verotoxin which is similar to EHEC verotoxin acts by inhibition of protein synthesis.
Option B: Inhibit the release of Ach: Botulinum toxin acts by inhibiting the release of ACH.
Option D: Inhibit the release of Norepinephrine: Release of norepinephrine is inhibited by certain drugs like alpha-2 agonists.

stem: Which of the following diseases is spread via all components of blood?
subject: Microbiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Malaria parasites (Plasmodium) are present Inside red blood cells Hence, transmission can occur through whole blood
• Also transmitted via packed RBCs Can be transmitted through platelet concentrates if contaminated with RBCs
• Even fresh frozen plasma may transmit malaria if not properly screened Therefore, malaria is known to be transmissible through all components of blood
• This makes malaria a classic example of a transfusion-transmitted Infection involving all blood components

Other Options:
Option A-Syphilis: • Caused by Treponema pallidum Transmitted mainly through sexual contact and transplacentally • Can be transmitted by fresh whole blood, but not all blood components Spirochetes do not survive well in stored blood • Incorrect
Option B-Toxoplasmosis: Transmitted by undercooked meat, cat feces, and transplacentally Blood transmission is rare Not transmitted via all blood components • Incorrect
Option D-Filarial Infection: Transmitted by mosquito vectors • Microfilariae may be present in blood, but transfusion transmission is uncommon Not transmitted through all blood components • Incorrect

stem: A female patient presents with swollen Bartholin glands. Discharging pus showed gram negative intracellular diplococci. Which is the organism?
subject: Microbiology, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
In women, the initial infection involves the urethra and the cervix uteri and it can extend to Bartholin's glands, the endometrium, and the fallopian tubes.
On microscopy, the Gram-negative diplococci present in pairs can be seen.

Other Options:
Option B: Chlamydia: Chlamydia appears as a cluster of spores like on microscopy.
Option C: Chancrold: The bacilli may be arranged in small groups or whorls or in parallel chains, giving a 'school of fish' or 'rallroad track' appearance.
Option D: Syphilis: Syphilis on dark microscopy shows thin forward and backward moving organisms.

explanation:
Solution
• Gastric MALToma is the most frequent MALT lymphoma that is caused by H. pylori.
Chlamydia psittaci is involved in causing MALT lymphoma of the eyes and tear ducts.
Campylobacter jejuni, commonly involved in food poisoning, is a causative agent of small bowel MALToma.
• Borrelia burgdorferi causes Lyme disease, which is involved in causing skin MALToma.

Other Options:
Option A, B, & C: These are incorrect options.

stem: All of the following are immunohistochemical markers for GIST, except?

explanation:
Solution
• Neuregulin/NRG-1 is not a marker for GIST diagnosis.
It is seen in non-TRU type lung adenocarcinoma, Including mucinous adenocarcinoma (most common subtype - 38.1%), followed by TRU type lung adenocarcinoma (23.8%) and Gl and PB adenocarcinoma (23.8%).

Other Options:
Options A, B, & C: PDGFR beta, SDH, and KIT are immunohistochemical markers for gastrointestinal stromal turnors (GIST).

stem: Which of the following instruments is not used in the colorimetric method for the estimation of hemoglobin?

Solution
The instrument shown in the image is an RBC pipette and it is not used in the colorimetric method for the estimation of hemoglobin.

Other Options:
Option A: The images in options A, B, & C show a comparator, hemoglobin tube, and a stirrer respectively. They are components of Sahll's haemoglobinometer set. Sahli's method of hemoglobin estimation is a colorimetric method.

stem: A 37 year old male was recently diagnosed with islet cell cancer. Which of the following is a new intranuclear immunohistochemistry marker for neuroendocrine tumors?
subject: Pathology
explanation:
Solution
INSM stands for Insulinoma-associated protein-1, a new marker for neuroendocrine tumors that is Intranuclear.

Other Options:
Options A, B and C are not intranuclear immunohistochemistry markers for neuroendocrine tumors.

stem: Which of the following features does not distinguish between necrosis and apoptosis?
subject: Pathology
explanation:
Solution
Underlying pathological condition is not an important feature of differentiation between necrosis and apoptosis as necrosis and apoptosis both can be triggered by an underlying disease/pathological condition.

Other Options:
Option A: In necrosis, cellular contents leak out and undergo enzymatic digestion, while in apoptosis, they're enclosed until released as apoptotic bodies, with the membrane Intact until engulfed by phagocytic cells.
Option C: In apoptosis, inflammation is rare, while in necrosis, it's common due to released cellular contents triggering inflammation.
Option D: in apoptosis, the cell membrane remains intact, whereas in necrosis, it is disrupted.

stem: Which of the following is not a feature of G6PD deficiency?
options: Males and females are equally affected
subject: Pathology, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
G6PD deficiency, being a recessive X-linked trait, puts males at a significantly higher risk of symptomatic disease compared to females.

Other Options:
Option B, C and D:
In G6PD deficiency, NADPH does not form, and the glutathione pathway does not occur.
• This leads to an increase in the concentration of free radicals (oxidative stress).
• Oxidative stress causes the denaturation of hemoglobin and leads to the formation of Heinz bodies.

stem: Which connective tissue disorder is not associated with aneurysm formation?
subject: Pathology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
Atherosclerosis is not a connective tissue disorder. However, it is associated with aneurysm formation. It occurs in hyperlipidemia, Inflammation, and hemodynamic disturbances.

Other Options:
Options A, B and D are connective tissue disorders associated with aneurysm formation.

stem:
Match the following blood products with their shelf lives:
Blood ProductShelf Life
